title: Is Tamil Nadu's Financial Position Heading for Bankruptcy?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All eight source pages were checked for debt totals, percentages, annual series, fiscal and revenue deficits, investment figures, quoted English reports, and the signature date. Clearly visible OCR character errors, accidental word breaks, and a stray table marker were corrected only in the reading copy. The numerical claims are preserved as printed and attributed in the translation to the speaker or publication that supplies them.
paragraphs: *Dinakaran* carried a news analysis under the title “Tamil Nadu Going Bankrupt.” Having read it, I wish to give you some statistics so that you too may understand, and explain to others, the debt burden and financial condition of the Tamil Nadu Government at the end of five years of Jayalalithaa's rule.

Jayalalithaa's five-year term is about to end, and the general election to the Legislative Assembly is approaching. At present, even without including the Electricity Board's debts, the Tamil Nadu Government's total debt is ₹2,11,483 crore. That means a debt burden of ₹28,778 rests upon the head of every person in Tamil Nadu. Under AIADMK rule, the debt has increased by nearly ₹1.5 lakh crore over what already existed.

To be more specific, when Jayalalithaa was previously in power from 2001 to 2006, the debt burden stood at 22.29 per cent of the State's Gross Domestic Product. During the DMK Government of 2006-2011, it fell to 19.58 per cent of GSDP.
Speaking in Chennai on 9 April 2011, Mr Pranab Mukherjee, then Finance Minister of India, praised the DMK Government's sound financial management:

> “Tamil Nadu is one of the few States in India that has satisfactorily maintained fiscal discipline and stability. The Tamil Nadu Government's debt is well below the prescribed limit. Not even for a single day has the Government lacked funds in its account and taken an overdraft from the Reserve Bank of India.”
But what do all the English newspapers say about the condition of today's AIADMK Government?

Within ten months of coming to power in 2011, after increasing milk prices and bus fares, they announced a single, steep 37 per cent increase in electricity tariffs. When Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister in 2001, she had raised electricity tariffs from 1 December 2001. Again, on 15 March 2003, she imposed tariff increases worth ₹1,398 crore. On that very day, she announced the cancellation of free electricity for agriculture and huts introduced by the DMK Government. She increased domestic electricity tariffs by 22.13 per cent and tariffs for educational institutions by 16.61 per cent.
Even after all that, what is Tamil Nadu's financial condition?
*The Hindu* reported:
> “It's official and out in the open. The State Government's financial health is quite bad. It was revealed in an innocuous statement by Chief Minister O. Panneerselvam.”
In other words, it was an official matter, but it had come into the open: the State Government's financial position was extremely poor.

In the Budget that O. Panneerselvam, then Chief Minister and now Finance Minister, read in the Assembly on 13 February 2014, page 68 said, “The revenue surplus in 2014-2015 is estimated at ₹289.36 crore.” But his estimate proved wrong. *The Hindu* reported that by the end of the first six months of that financial year, the Tamil Nadu Government had a revenue deficit of ₹7,652 crore.

The Government's tax revenue ordinarily accounts for more than two-thirds of total revenue. But its performance from April to September 2014 showed that revenue had not arrived at the expected level. Against an estimate of ₹91,835 crore in the State Government's own tax revenue, only ₹37,056 crore was received during that period.

Statistics for the different components of State tax revenue show that no department earned as much as expected. Against expected growth of 20.3 per cent in State revenue, only 14.6 per cent was achieved. Against an expectation of 49 per cent in revenue through Central taxes and Union Government allocations, receipts amounted to only 37.68 per cent.
*The Hindu* had already stripped bare the AIADMK Government's financial condition:

> “The early signs of the declining financial health were visible even during the previous year (2013-2014) when the State registered a revenue deficit of about Rs. 1,790 crore, as per supplementary accounts. The revenue deficit came after two successive years of revenue surplus.”
To say more, journalist S.A.M. Barakath Ali wrote an explanatory article in *Junior Vikatan* under the title “Tamil Nadu Government in Financial Crisis.” It said:

> “The Finance Department is stumbling, unable to reach any target. The Government devoted its greatest attention to increasing TASMAC revenue. Had it displayed that degree of administrative ability in other departments, total revenue would have increased. Government lethargy is the reason for this situation. > > “The Government has no way to manage it except by borrowing. Tamil Nadu is already struggling under debt of ₹1.80 lakh crore. This will affect the State's overall growth. The Government has taken no firm steps to collect old tax arrears or prevent tax evasion. Apart from the Tamil Nadu Government's debt, the Electricity Board owes ₹1.5 lakh crore. If the debts of public-sector undertakings are added, Tamil Nadu's total debt burden will exceed ₹4 lakh crore.”
*Junior Vikatan* reported this at length, attributing it to Finance Department sources.

What became of the many announcements in previous Budgets: a ₹3,833.62 crore grand project to restore the Cooum River; ₹5,166 crore for river-linking and flood-prevention works; a ₹8,350 crore monorail project along three corridors; a ₹1,075 crore second-phase Outer Ring Road connecting Nemilichery and Minjur; an integrated knowledge park at Chennai's DPI campus; and new lakes with a capacity of one TMC each at Thervoy Kandigai, Thirukandalam and Ramanjeri? Did they all fly away on the winds of publicity?
Why, Tamil Nadu Government employees receive a Pongal bonus every year. With only two weeks remaining before the Tamil festival of Pongal, the Government must immediately announce the bonus.
On 13 February 2014, O. Panneerselvam, then Chief Minister, read the AIADMK Government's Budget in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly. On page 31, he said:

> “I am pleased to inform this august House that, to give a further impetus to industrial growth and infrastructure in our State, this Government will hold an International Investors Meet in Chennai this coming October. An allocation of ₹100 crore has been made for this purpose in this Budget.”
That announcement was read in the Budget in February 2014. But was the Investors Meet held in October as the Minister told the Assembly? No.

As early as 2012, they made a colossal announcement that MoUs would be signed with twelve companies in a single day, that factories would begin with investment of ₹20,925 crore, and that 36,855 people would thereby obtain employment. Every newspaper carried the report prominently.

We asked the Government to tell the people whether the investments of those twelve companies had arrived in Tamil Nadu, whether they had started their industries, and whether 36,855 people had obtained jobs in them. The rulers did not open their mouths. How could their tongues move when nothing had happened?

On another occasion, reports said that agreements worth ₹5,081 crore had been signed with sixteen companies, but that the signatory companies had still not begun production. The following year's Budget also said that MoUs had been signed for total investment of ₹26,625 crore, but that only ₹10,660 crore had actually been invested, creating 10,022 additional jobs.

After this, then Chief Minister Panneerselvam announced that the Investors Meet would be held in May 2015. With only one year remaining before the Legislative Assembly general election, I asked at the time what benefit could come from holding the conference in May 2015. There was no answer to that either.

In connection with the conference scheduled for May, they advertised on a massive scale that agreements worth ₹18,000 crore would be signed with seven companies at a preliminary conference in Chennai on 13 February 2015. But did that event take place there?

It was said that the Investors Meet kept being postponed so that Jayalalithaa could be acquitted in the disproportionate-assets appeal, resume the Chief Minister's office, and conduct the conference while in that post. As though proving this true, the conference was held only after Jayalalithaa returned as Chief Minister.
But did industries involving ₹2.42 lakh crore in investment arrive as Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced at that conference? The painful answer is no.

At a time when four years of AIADMK rule had ended and its fifth year was under way, the English daily *The Times of India* published a long illustrated article showing how deeply these rulers had trapped the people of Tamil Nadu in debt. Its headline was:
> **How Tamil Nadu Government Debt Keeps People in Red? With Interest Payments Climbing, State Has Little Left to Help the Poor Who Depend on Moneylenders**
That is, as the interest payable by the Government keeps rising, the State has only a meagre amount left to help poor people dependent on moneylenders.
Debt stood at approximately ₹90,000 crore during the DMK Government. It now stands at ₹2,11,483 crore, an increase to 234.98 per cent of the earlier level.
Look at Tamil Nadu's debt year by year:

It is not I but *The Times of India* that wrote how enormously debt had increased under AIADMK rule. In other words, compared with the DMK Government, debt under the AIADMK Government had increased by more than 200 per cent.
The Budget during the DMK Government estimated that in 2010-2011 the revenue deficit would be ₹3,396.45 crore, the fiscal deficit ₹16,222.13 crore, and the fiscal deficit 3.72 per cent of GSDP.

After four years of AIADMK rule, have either the revenue deficit or the fiscal deficit fallen below their levels during the DMK Government? Last year's Budget estimated a revenue deficit of ₹4,616.02 crore and a fiscal deficit of ₹31,829.19 crore for 2015-2016. Compared with the DMK Government, the revenue deficit under AIADMK rule had increased by ₹1,219.57 crore and the fiscal deficit by ₹15,607.06 crore.

The debt burden has swollen under AIADMK rule. Both the revenue deficit and fiscal deficit have increased, leaving Tamil Nadu afflicted by the disease called “deficit.” Government charges have risen, while the prices of essential commodities have climbed to the skies. The natural deluge combined with a man-made flood to turn people's lives upside down.
The livelihoods of poor and middle-class people have become a question mark, compelling them to wait for dawn. Tamil Nadu's financial position is speeding towards bankruptcy!
Is this the miserable achievement of five years of AIADMK rule?
With affection,
M.K.
31 December 2015
